description:
To properly set the group owner of /etc/shells, run the command: 
$ sudo chgrp root /etc/shells
rationale:
The <tt>/etc/shells</tt> file contains the list of full pathnames to shells on the system.
Since this file is used by many system programs this file should be protected.
ocil_clause:
/etc/shells does not have a group owner of
root
ocil:
To check the group ownership of /etc/shells, run the command: 
$ ls -lL /etc/shells
If properly configured, the output should indicate the following group-owner: root
fixtext:
Change the group of the file /etc/shells to root by running the following command:
$ sudo chgrp root /etc/shells
srg_requirement: The Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 /etc/shells file must have a group-owner of root to prevent unauthorized access.
title: Verify Group Who Owns /etc/shells File
